blocks.content: She cried.

Not the polite kind — the real kind, the kind that comes from somewhere deep and doesn't ask permission. She had held it through the ceremony and through the reception and through two days of driving and she had held it when they walked in the door and she had held it through her mother's words about grace and carrying and living fully — and then her father's line arrived: I trust you with her. I want you to understand the weight of that sentence.
That was the one that got through.

Because she knew what it had cost him to write it. Because she knew her father — the way he loved quietly and showed it through preparation and research and forty-one years of the same fireplace — and she knew what it meant for him to say that plainly, in ink, on a card he would leave in a room he'd been happy in. To a man he was trusting with the most important thing he had.
She put one hand over her mouth and stood at the counter and let it come.

Ethan crossed to her immediately. He didn't say anything. He just put his arms around her and held on — not trying to stop it, not trying to comfort it away, just being there while it moved through her the way it needed to.
She cried for her parents, forty-one years ago in this room.

She cried because her mother had seen her. Really seen her — not the version of her that managed and held things together and said she was fine, but the real one, the one who carried something hard and kept walking anyway. Her mother had watched and known and had waited until this exact room to say so.
She cried because she was standing in the place where it all began for them, and she was still here, and she had made it to this.
It took a few minutes. Ethan held her through all of it.
When it passed she pulled back and looked at him — eyes red, not embarrassed about it.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Read it.
She held out the card. He took it.

She watched him read. She saw the moment he reached his section — not the slight change she had described before, but something more than that. Something in him went very quiet and very serious, the way he went quiet when he was in front of something that required his full attention.
He read his section twice. Maybe three times.
Then he lowered the card slowly and stood there for a moment, looking at nothing, just holding what he had read.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He trusted me with you.
He said it quietly. Not to her, exactly — more like he was saying it aloud so he could hear it properly.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: He put it in writing. He wanted me to know the weight of it.
He looked up at her.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: I'm not going to let him down. I'm not going to let you down. I want you to know that isn't something I'm saying because the moment calls for it — it is the thing I intend to do every day for the rest of our lives. Whatever that looks like. However long we get.
She looked at him for a long moment.
She believed him. Not because he said it — she had believed it before he said it. She believed it because of the way he had read that card. Because of the way he was still holding it.
He set it gently on the counter — careful with it, the way you are careful with something that now belongs to both of you.
He put his arm around her. She leaned into it.
Forty-one years ago, her parents had stood in this room.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: They were so happy here.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: We're going to be happy here too.
He said it back to her this time. The same way she had said true things to him — not wishing, not hoping.
Stating.
